text: The pancreatic duodenectomy, or Whipple procedure, is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of pancreatic cancer when the tumor is located in the head of the pancreas. During the procedure, we remove the first portion of the small intestine, called the duodenum, we remove the bottom half of the bile duct, and we remove approximately half of the pancreas. We also need to remove any surrounding lymph nodes. The small bowel is then brought up and connected to the pancreas, bile duct, and stomach to restore intestinal continuity.

At TriHealth, we can often perform this surgical procedure minimally invasively with the use of robotic technology. The following video is a real Whipple procedure performed at TriHealth. The video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical Whipple procedure. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how this surgery is performed. If you are scheduled to have this surgery, your surgery may be performed in a similar fashion.

So the first portion of the procedure is simply exposing the pancreas so that we can do our resection, which we call a Whipple procedure. Many people ask, why is this called a Whipple procedure? And the origin of that is the technical name for the procedure is pancreatic duodenectomy. It means pancreatic, which is pancreas, duodenal, which is duodenum, or the first portion of the small intestine. and then ectomy means to remove. So the full name of the procedure means to remove the pancreas and duodenum.

Obviously that's a really long name and so we've shortened it to the Whipple procedure because that is the name of the surgeon who really pioneered this procedure. His name was Alan Oldfather Whipple and he was an American surgeon who practiced in the early nineteen hundreds and in the nineteen thirties he started looking into how he could remove tumors in the head of the pancreas. I mean, he came up with this procedure. And so, obviously, it's been nearly a century since we've been doing this. Dr. Whipple actually only performed thirty-seven of these in his entire career. At Tri-Health, we do more than thirty-seven in one year alone. And so, like anything you do, the more you do of it, the better you're gonna get at it.

The surgeon lifts the yellow pancreatic tissue, exposing the purple portal vein running behind it. The portal vein is carefully dissected off the back of the pancreas to ensure it is not too adherent, allowing the procedure to continue.

The surgeon retracts the stomach to expose the first portion of the small intestine, known as the duodenum. The duodenum is being separated from surrounding tissues, including the right kidney visible behind it. The surgeon continues to divide attachments between the duodenum and the retroperitoneum, the tissue surrounding the posterior abdominal structures.

The surgeon disconnects tissue attachments to free the duodenum for removal, as the head of the pancreas is adherent to it. The inferior vena cava, a large vein visible behind the duodenum, is carefully separated from the duodenum during this process.

The surgeon performs a portal dissection, focusing on the portal triad visible at the top left of the screen. This includes the portal vein, common bile duct, and hepatic artery. The common bile duct, which drains bile from the liver to the intestine, is identified for division as it passes through the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the right gastric artery and prepares to divide it. The hepatic artery is visible, along with accompanying lymph nodes that need to be removed for cancer staging. The gastroduodenal artery will be located and divided later in the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the hepatic artery extending towards the liver, with branches including the gastroduodenal artery. The surrounding tissue is carefully dissected to skeletonize the gastroduodenal artery, allowing for clear visualization and preparation for division.

The surgeon isolates the gastroduodenal artery, ensuring liver perfusion by clamping the vessel and injecting ICG dye, visible with a special camera. Clips are used to ligate the artery, followed by division with scissors. The procedure progresses to a pyloric-preserving Whipple, leaving the stomach intact while preparing to navigate around the stomach and duodenum.

The surgeon divides the stomach and pushes it aside, then lifts the pancreas off the portal vein at the neck region using umbilical tape. The pancreas is shaped like a fish, with a head, body, and tail. The area to the left of the neck will be removed, while the right side will remain functional. The surgeon isolates the common bile duct at the porta hepatis, noting a replaced right hepatic artery behind it, which must be preserved to maintain blood flow to the liver. Attention is then turned to the small bowel, identifying the duodenum's exit from the pancreas, where it will be divided.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the attachments of the small bowel, ensuring it is free for removal. The portion of the bowel to the right of the stapler remains in the patient for reconstruction, to be connected to the pancreas and bile duct to maintain normal bile and pancreatic juice flow.

The surgeon separates the small bowel and pulls it to the opposite side of the abdomen. Using scissors and cautery, the pancreas is divided, minimizing bleeding. The pancreas is then rolled off the portal vein, and attention is redirected to the bile duct.

The surgeon divides the bile duct while carefully preserving the replaced right hepatic artery. Using a hook, clips are applied to secure the vessel before dividing it with a vessel sealer. The pancreas is further separated from the portal vein, which transitions into the superior mesenteric vein. The first jejunal branch is identified and preserved, while other small branches are divided. The pancreas is then detached from the superior mesenteric artery and portal vein, freeing the specimen. The specimen is placed in an endo-pouch for safe removal and sent to pathology for margin analysis.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase by bringing the previously divided small bowel up behind the colon into the right abdomen, positioning it adjacent to the pancreas and bile duct. The first anastomosis involves connecting the pancreas to the small bowel by opening the bowel and inserting the cut end of the pancreas into it.

The surgeon performs a pancreatic jejunal invagination by sewing the pancreas to the small bowel. A hole is made in the small bowel, matching the size of the pancreas, allowing the cut end of the pancreas to be inserted into the small bowel. This setup facilitates the drainage of pancreatic fluid into the small intestine, where peristalsis moves the fluid along the bowel to mix with stomach contents.

The surgeon completes the back row of sutures securing the pancreas to the bowel and begins the front row, ensuring the pancreas can drain into the bowel. A drain is placed to collect any fluid from the anastomosis site to prevent leaks.

The surgeon completes sewing the front wall of the anastomosis between the pancreas and the small bowel. The procedure progresses to the anastomosis between the common bile duct and the small bowel, ensuring there is no tension in the connection. The gallbladder is removed as part of the Whipple procedure due to its non-functionality post-surgery.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to detach the gallbladder from the liver, isolating the cystic duct and cystic artery. Clips are applied to the cystic duct and artery before dividing them, allowing for the removal of the gallbladder. The surgical field is prepared for the anastomosis between the common bile duct and the small bowel, ensuring the connection is secure to minimize leakage risk.
Thank you.

The surgeon completes the bile duct anastomosis and proceeds to perform the anastomosis between the stomach and the small intestine. The pylorus muscle is visible from the inside of the duodenum, and the connection is made just beyond this muscle to preserve stomach function. The staple line from the divided duodenum is removed, and the stomach is sewn to the small bowel in two layers to minimize the risk of leakage.

The surgeon completes the first layer of sutures on the front wall of the anastomosis between the pancreas and the small bowel, then adds a second layer of stitches. After finishing the anastomosis, a drain is placed near both the pancreas and bile duct anastomoses to manage any potential leaks.
